Farn Polrrrcrl Pnacucps CouvrrssroN
P.O. Box tt07 . 42tt J Strec't . Sucramento. CA 9-5till-0U07
(916) 322--t660 . Fax (916) -322-0tt86
July 25,2000
Juanda Lowder Daniel
Deputy City Attorney
City of Riverside
3900 Main Street
Riverside, California 92522
Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-00-134
Dear Ms. Daniel:
This letter is in response to your request for advice on behalf of Riverside City
Councilmember Maureen Kane about the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform
Act (the "Act").r
QUESTIONS
1. Does Councilmember Kane's involvement in the development of a tract of land, part
of which is owned by her, constitute an interest in a business entity for purposes of the Act?
2. If so, does Councilmember Kane's interest in the business entity create a conflict of
interest that would preclude her from participating in the decision to approve an EIR and a permit
in connection with a residential development project located near the tract?
CONCLUSIONS
1. Yes. The joint development of property rises to the level of a joint venture and, as
such, is a business entity as defined in the Act. As a partner in the joint venture, the
councilmember has an economic interest in the business entity for conflict-of-interest purposes
I Govemment Code sections 81000 - 91015. Commission regulations appear attitle2, sections 18109 -
18996, of the California Code of Regulations.


File No. A-00-134
Page 2
2. The councilmember will have a conflict of interest in a decision relating to the
Hawarden project if the decision will affect the fair market value of the five parcels that are
assets of the joint venture by $10,000 or more, or have any other material financial effect on the
joint venture as described below.
FACTS
The city council is considering an application for a permit to establish a planned
residential development on72 acres of land characterizedby significant rock outcroppings and
steep slopes (referred to as the "Hawarden Project"). The project is located in the residential
conservation zone.
Councilmember Kane owns real property consisting of significant acreage situated
approximately 3,200 feet west of the Hawarden Project. There are at least ten parcels with 2,500
feet of the councilmember's real property.
In addition, the councilmember and the owners of four large acreage parcels surrounding
her property jointly applied to the city for atractmap ("Tract240l6") to subdivide and develop
the property. Councilmember Kane has no direct ownership interest in the other four
surrounding parcels. However, once the city approves a final subdivision map, all five of the
joint venturers will have an economic interest in the combined properties. The easterly most
boundary of Tract 24016 is located within 2,500 feet of the Hawarden Project.
The Hawarden Project is coming before the city council for discussion concerning the
scope of the Environmental lmpact Report ("ER"). Upon completion of the EIR, the Hawarden
applicant will request certification of the EIR" and approval of the project. The councilmember's
project will not be contingent upon the approval or denial of the Hawarden project. However,
due to their proximity, decisions about the Hawarden project may influence decisions regarding
the councilmember's development proj ect.
ANALYSIS
A. Conflict-of-Interest Prohibition
A public official may not make, participate in making, or in any way attempt to use his or
her official position to influence a govemmental decision in which the official knows, or has
reason to know, that he or she has a financial interest. (Section 87100.) The Commission has
developed an eight-step approach for determining whether an individual has a disqualiffing
financial interest in a decision. (Regulation 18700(b).)


File No. A-00-134
Page 3
1. Public Official
The conflict-of-interest prohibition only applies to public officials. (Section 87100.) As
a member of the city council, Councilmember Kane is a "public official" subject to the
prohibition. (Section 82048.)
2. Conduct Covered
The prohibition covers specific conduct: making, participating in making, or attempting
to use one's official position to influence a govemmental decision. (Section 87100.) These
terms are defined in Regulations 18702-18702.4. By voting and deliberating on an EIR and a
proposed permit for a planned residential development, the councilmember will be engaging in
conduct regulated by the Act. @egulation 18702.1.)
3. Economic Interest
A public official has a disqualiffing financial interest in a decision if it is reasonably
foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect, distinguishable from its effect
on the public generally, on the official,2 or on the following enumerated economic interests:
1. Any business entity in which the official has a direct or indirect investment worth
$1,000 or more. (Section 87103(a).)
2. ky real property in which the official has a direct or indirect interest worth $1,000 or
more. (Section 87103(b).)
3. Any source of income of $250 or more provided to the official within 12 months
before the decision. (Section 87103(c).)
4. Arry business entity in which the official is a director, officer, partner, trustee,
employee, or holds any position of management. (Section 87103(d).)
5. Any donor of gifts worth $300 or more provided to the official within 12 months
before the decision. (Section 87103(e).)
a. Economic Interest in Joint Venture
The term "business entity" includes a joint venture. (Section 82005.) The common
definition of a'Joint venture" is a "special combination of two or more persons, where in some
specific venture, a profit is jointly sought without any actual partnership or corporate
2 A decision will have a financial effect "on the official," within the meaning of Section 87103, if the
decision will affect the official's personal expenses, income, assets, or liabilities, or those of his or her immediate
family. (Regulation 18703.5.) This is known as the "personal financial effect" rule. When determining whether the
rule applies in a given situation, financial effects on business entities and real properfy in which a public official has
an ownership interest are not considered, since such interests are separately analyzedas possible conflicts. (Ibid)


File No. A-00-134
Page 4
designation, or as an association of persons to carry out a single business enterprise for profit, for
which they combine their property, money, effects, skill, and knowledge." (Sadugor v. Holstein
(1962) 199 Cal.App.2d 477, 483.)
Along with four other property owners, Councilmember Kane has jointly applied to the
city for a tract map to subdivide and develop five adjacent parcels. We consider this joint
enterprise for profit to be a joint venture. As a venturer, Councilmember Kane has an economic
interest in a business entity for purposes of the Act. (Section 87103(a) and (d).)
b. Economic Interest in Partners of the Joint Venture
In some cases, a public official may have an economic interest in a business partner. In a
formal opinion, In re Nord (1983) 8 FPPC Ops. 6, the Commission held that a limited partner
had an investment interest in the general partner of a limited partnership. In making that
determination, the Commission reasoned that when a limited parhrer invests money in a limited
partnership, the limited partner is actually investing in the entrepreneurial skills of the general
partner, who ordinarily has the sole discretion and authority to manage the investment. Further,
in a footnote of that opinion, the Commission noted that its decision would also apply to a
partner in a partnership with two equal partners. (In re Nord, supra, fu. 16.) However, we have
advised that the Nord opinion does not apply to partnerships that have more than two partners if
no single partner holds a controlling position or acts as a managing parhrer. (Hahn Advice
Letter, No. I-91-311.) Since the councilmember's joint venture has five partners, it does not
appear that the Nord opinion applies. As such, Councilmember Kane does not have an economic
interest in the other property owners who are partners in the joint venfure.
c. Economic Interest in Real Property
Councilmember Kane has an economic interest in the real property that she is
contributing to the joint venture. (Section 87103(b).) She also has an economic interest in the
other four parcels that are part of the joint venture. You indicate that Councilmember Kane and
the other four properfy owners have agreed to combine their properties to develop the property as
one tract of land. By doing so, all five properfy owners are contributing their properties to the
joint venture.3 Under the Act, an "interest in real property" includes a pro rata share of real
property of any trust in which the official owns a 10 percent interest or greater. (Section 82033.)
In common law, the title of a partner in joint venture assets is that of a beneficiary of a
constructive trust. (Rosen v. E.C. Losch Company, Inc. (1965) 234 Cal.App.zd 324,335.)
Accordingly, we conclude that, as a joint venturer, Councilmember Kane has a beneficial
economic interest in the other four properties that are assets of the joint venture.
3 See Gross v. Raeburn (1963) 219 Cal.App.2d792,799 (holding that statute of frauds does not apply
when property owners orally agree to combine their properties and form a joint venture.)


File No. A-00-134
Page 5
Once a public official identifies his or her relevant economic interests, the official must
evaluate whether it is reasonably foreseeable that a decision will have a material financial effect
on one or more of those economic interests. This determination takes three steps which are
discussed in detail below as Steps 4, 5 and 6. First, the official must determine whether the
economic interest will be directly or indirectly involved in the decision. (Regulation
18700(bX4).) Based upon the type of involvement, the official must then find the applicable
materiality standard set forth in Commission regulations. (Regulation 18700(b)(5).) After
finding the applicable materiality standard, the official must then decide whether it is reasonably
foreseeable that the standard will be met. (Regulation 18700(b)(6).)
4. Direct or Indirect Involvement
a. Business Interest
A person, including a business entity, is directly involved in a decision if the person
initiates, is a named party in, or the subject of, the decision. (Regulation 18704.1(a)(2).) A
person is the "subject" of the decision if the decision involves the issuance, renewal, approval,
denial or revocation of any license, permit, or other entitlement to or contract with the person.
Qbid.) If a person is not directly involved in a decision, that person is indirectly involved for
purposes of finding the applicable materiality standard. (Regulation 18704.1(b).)
Neither the joint venture, nor any of its individual members, is a named party in, or the
subjects of, the decision to approve the Hawarden Project. Therefore, the joint venture is
indirectly involved in the decision.
b. Real Property Interest
Real property is directly involved in a decision when the decision involves: (1) the
zoring, sale, lease, or boundary change of the property; (2) a license, permit or other land use
entitlement authorizing a specific use of the property; (3) taxes or fees to be assessed on the
property; or (4) redevelopment, and the properly is located in the redevelopment area.
(Regulation 1870a.2@).) If real property is not directly involved in a decision, it is indirectly
involved for purposes of finding the applicable materiality standard. (Regulation 18704.2(b).)
Here, the councilmember's real property interests are indirectly involved in the decision
to approve a nearby development project.


File No. A-00-134
Page 6
5. Applicable Materiality Standard
a. Business Interest
Regulation 18705.1(b) sets forth the materiality standards for business entities that are
indirectly involved in a decision. The standards in the regulation vary depending upon the size
of the business entity. The bigger the business entity, the greater the monetary impact must be in
order for the effect to be material.
Presumably, the joint venture is a small business entity as described in Regulation
18705.1(b)(7).4 For such businesses, the effect of a decision is material if it will result in an
increase or decrease in the entity's: (1) gross revenues of $10,000 or more in a fiscal year; (2)
existing expenses of $2,500 or more in a fiscal year; or (3) value of assets or liabilities of
$10,000 or more. (Regulation 18705.1(bX7).)
b. Real Property Interest
Regulation 18705.2 sets forth the materiality standards that apply to economic interests in
real property. The materiality thresholds vary depending upon the distance between the official's
real property and the real property that is the subject of the decision.
(1) Councilmember Kanets Real Property - Councilmember Kane's property is more
than 2,500 feet away from the Hawarden project. When an official's property falls outside a
2,500-foot radius from the boundaries of the proposed project, any financial effect on the
property is not considered material unless BOTH of the following apply: (1) it is reasonably
foreseeable that the project will affect the fair market value of the official's property by $10,000,
or the arurual rental value by $1,000, and (2) the effect will not be substantially the same as the
effect on at least 25 percent of all the properties within 2,500 feet of the official's property; or
there are not at least 10 properties under separate ownership within 2,500 feet of the official's
property. (Regulation 1 8705.2(bX2).)
(2) Joint Venture's Real Property - One of the five properties that is part of the joint
venture falls within 2,500 feet of the Hawarden project. For property interests that are between
300 and 2,500 feet from the boundaries of a proposed project, the effect of the decision will be
material if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will increase or decrease: (1) the fair
market value of the property by $10,000, or (2) the annual rental value of the property by $1,000
(Regulation 1 870s.2(b)( 1 XC).)
a Regulation I 8705. I (bX7) business entities are entities that are not listed on the Pacific Stock Exchange
or the Eligible Securities List maintained by the Departrnent of Corporations, and that do meet the qualifications for
listing on the NASDAQ Stock Exchange. (Regulation 18705.1(b).)


File No. A-00-134
Page 7
6. Foreseeability
Once the official finds the relevant materiality standard, the official must determine
whether it is reasonably foreseeable that the standard will be met as a result of the decision. A
financial effect is considered reasonably foreseeable at the time a govemmental decision is made
if there is a substantial likelihood that it will occur. @egulation 18706.) A material financial
effect need not be a certainty as a result of the decision, but it must be more than a mere
possibility. (In re Thorner (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. l9S.)
Consequently, the question is whether it is substantially likely that the decision to
approve the EIR for the Hawarden project will have a material financial effect on any of
Councilmember Kane's relevant economic interests.
a. Councilmember Kane's Economic Interest in Joint Venture
As to the joint venture, the specific question is whether it is substantially likely that the
decision will increase or decrease the entity's: (1) gross revenues by $10,000 or more in a fiscal
year; (2) existing expenses by $2,500 or more in a fiscal year; or (3) value of assets or liabilities
by $10,000 or more. (Regulation 18705.1(bX7).)
To answer this question, Councilmember Kane must consider whether the Hawarden
project will materially affect the fair market value of any of the five parcels that are part of the
joint venfure, since these properties are "assets" of the joint venfure. ln addition, she must also
consider whether the project will affect the number of residential units that the joint venture
plans to build, and, if so, whether the resulting effect on the entity's gross income would be
material. (See Willians Advice Letter, No. A-00-036, copy enclosed.)
b. Councilmember Kane's Economic Interest in Her Real Property
Regarding her interest in real property, Councilmember Kane must determine whether
there are special circumstances about the Hawarden project which make it substantially likely
that the decision to approve the project will affect the fair market value of her property by
$10,000 or more, or the rental value of her property by $1,000 or more in a l2-month period.
(Regulation 18705.2(b)(2).) If such circumstances are present, the effect will be material unless:
(1) the effect of the decision will be substantially the same as the effect upon at least 25 percent
of all properties which are within a 2,500-foot radius of the boundaries of her home; or (2) there
are at least 10 properties under separate ownership within a 2,500-foot radius of her home.
You indicate that there are at least ten properties under separate ownership within 2,500
feet from the councilmember's property. Therefore, she does not have a conflict of interest in
decisions relating to the Hawarden project because of her real property interest.


File No. A-00-134
Page 8
c. Councilmember Kane's Economic Interest in Joint Venture Property
For her beneficial interest in the real property contributed to the joint venture that is
within 2,500 feet of the Hawarden project, Councilmember Kane must determine whether it is
substantially likely that approval of the project will increase or decrease the fair market value of
the real property by $10,000, or the annual rental value of the property by $1,000. (Regulation
18705.2O)(1XC).) If this effect is substantially likely, she will have a conflict of interest in the
project decision.
7. Public Generally Exception
An official who otherwise has a conflict of interest in a decision may still participate in
the decision if the'public generally" exception applies. (Section 87103.) For this exception to
apply, the decision must affect the official's economic interests in substantially the same manner
as it would affect a significant segment of the public. (Regulation 18707.)
Applying the exception is a two-step process. First, the official must determine whether a
"significant segment" of the public will be affected by the decision. Second, he or she must
determine whether this significant segment will be affected in "substantially the same manner" as
the official. When applying the two steps, real property and business interests must be analyzed,
separately. In addition, when an official has multiple economic interests that are the basis for his
or her disqualification in a decision, the official may participate in the decision under the public
generally exception only if the exception applies to each interest.
For an economic interest in a business entity, a significant segment may include 50
percent of all businesses in the official's jurisdiction or district. (Regulation 18707(bX1XB).)
Councilmember Kane is elected at large; therefore, her district is the entire city. You have not
provided any information regarding the number of businesses in the city that will be affected by
the Hawarden project. Therefore, we cannot determine whether the pubtic generally exception
will apply to the councilmember's economic interest in the joint venture.
For an economic interest in real property, a significant segment may include: (1) 10
percent or more of the population of the official's jurisdiction or district; (2) 10 percent or more
of all property owners, homeowners or households in the official's jurisdiction or district; or (3)
5,000 individual residents in the official's jurisdiction or district. (Regulation 18707(bX1XA).)
You have not indicated that ten percent of all the property owners in the city will be affected in
substantially the same manner as Councilmember Kane. As such, the public generally exception
does not apply to her real property interests.


File No. A-00-134
Page 9
8. Legally Required Participation
The eighth step pertains to the "legally required participation" rule. (See Regulation
18708.) This rule applies only in rare cases where several public officials in the same agency are
simultaneously disqualified. It does not appear that legally required participation rule is relevant
to your request.
If you have other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.
Sincerely,
Luisa Menchaca
Assistant General Cou4sel
By: Julia Bilaver
Staff Counsel, Legal Division

sections.question:
1. Does Councilmember Kane's involvement in the development of a tract of land, part
of which is owned by her, constitute an interest in a business entity for purposes of the Act?
2. If so, does Councilmember Kane's interest in the business entity create a conflict of
interest that would preclude her from participating in the decision to approve an EIR and a permit
in connection with a residential development project located near the tract?
sections.conclusion:
1. Yes. The joint development of property rises to the level of a joint venture and, as
such, is a business entity as defined in the Act. As a partner in the joint venture, the
councilmember has an economic interest in the business entity for conflict-of-interest purposes
2. The councilmember will have a conflict of interest in a decision relating to the
Hawarden project if the decision will affect the fair market value of the five parcels that are
assets of the joint venture by $10,000 or more, or have any other material financial effect on the
joint venture as described below.
sections.facts:
The city council is considering an application for a permit to establish a planned
residential development on72 acres of land characterizedby significant rock outcroppings and
steep slopes (referred to as the "Hawarden Project"). The project is located in the residential
conservation zone.
Councilmember Kane owns real property consisting of significant acreage situated
approximately 3,200 feet west of the Hawarden Project. There are at least ten parcels with 2,500

In addition, the councilmember and the owners of four large acreage parcels surrounding

sections.analysis:
A. Conflict-of-Interest Prohibition
A public official may not make, participate in making, or in any way attempt to use his or
her official position to influence a govemmental decision in which the official knows, or has
reason to know, that he or she has a financial interest. (Section 87100.) The Commission has
developed an eight-step approach for determining whether an individual has a disqualiffing
financial interest in a decision. (Regulation 18700(b).)
1. Public Official
The conflict-of-interest prohibition only applies to public officials. (Section 87100.) As
a member of the city council, Councilmember Kane is a "public official" subject to the
prohibition. (Section 82048.)
2. Conduct Covered
The prohibition covers specific conduct: making, participating in making, or attempting
to use one's official position to influence a govemmental decision. (Section 87100.) These
terms are defined in Regulations 18702-18702.4. By voting and deliberating on an EIR and a
proposed permit for a planned residential development, the councilmember will be engaging in
conduct regulated by the Act. @egulation 18702.1.)
3. Economic Interest
A public official has a disqualiffing financial interest in a decision if it is reasonably
foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect, distinguishable from its effect
on the public generally, on the official,2 or on the following enumerated economic interests:
1. Any business entity in which the official has a direct or indirect investment worth
$1,000 or more. (Section 87103(a).)
2. ky real property in which the official has a direct or indirect interest worth $1,000 or
more. (Section 87103(b).)
3. Any source of income of $250 or more provided to the official within 12 months
before the decision. (Section 87103(c).)
4. Arry business entity in which the official is a director, officer, partner, trustee,
employee, or holds any position of management. (Section 87103(d).)
5. Any donor of gifts worth $300 or more provided to the official within 12 months
before the decision. (Section 87103(e).)
a. Economic Interest in Joint Venture
The term "business entity" includes a joint venture. (Section 82005.) The common
definition of a'Joint venture" is a "special combination of two or more persons, where in some
specific venture, a profit is jointly sought without any actual partnership or corporate
2 A decision will have a financial effect "on the official," within the meaning of Section 87103, if the
decision will affect the official's personal expenses, income, assets, or liabilities, or those of his or her immediate
family. (Regulation 18703.5.) This is known as the "personal financial effect" rule. When determining whether the
rule applies in a given situation, financial effects on business entities and real properfy in which a public official has
an ownership interest are not considered, since such interests are separately analyzedas possible conflicts. (Ibid)
designation, or as an association of persons to carry out a single business enterprise for profit, for
which they combine their property, money, effects, skill, and knowledge." (Sadugor v. Holstein
(1962) 199 Cal.App.2d 477, 483.)
Along with four other property owners, Councilmember Kane has jointly applied to the
city for a tract map to subdivide and develop five adjacent parcels. We consider this joint
enterprise for profit to be a joint venture. As a venturer, Councilmember Kane has an economic
interest in a business entity for purposes of the Act. (Section 87103(a) and (d).)
b. Economic Interest in Partners of the Joint Venture
In some cases, a public official may have an economic interest in a business partner. In a
formal opinion, In re Nord (1983) 8 FPPC Ops. 6, the Commission held that a limited partner
had an investment interest in the general partner of a limited partnership. In making that
determination, the Commission reasoned that when a limited parhrer invests money in a limited
partnership, the limited partner is actually investing in the entrepreneurial skills of the general
partner, who ordinarily has the sole discretion and authority to manage the investment. Further,
in a footnote of that opinion, the Commission noted that its decision would also apply to a
partner in a partnership with two equal partners. (In re Nord, supra, fu. 16.) However, we have
advised that the Nord opinion does not apply to partnerships that have more than two partners if
no single partner holds a controlling position or acts as a managing parhrer. (Hahn Advice
Letter, No. I-91-311.) Since the councilmember's joint venture has five partners, it does not
appear that the Nord opinion applies. As such, Councilmember Kane does not have an economic
interest in the other property owners who are partners in the joint venfure.
c. Economic Interest in Real Property
Councilmember Kane has an economic interest in the real property that she is
contributing to the joint venture. (Section 87103(b).) She also has an economic interest in the
other four parcels that are part of the joint venture. You indicate that Councilmember Kane and
the other four properfy owners have agreed to combine their properties to develop the property as
one tract of land. By doing so, all five properfy owners are contributing their properties to the
joint venture.3 Under the Act, an "interest in real property" includes a pro rata share of real
property of any trust in which the official owns a 10 percent interest or greater. (Section 82033.)
In common law, the title of a partner in joint venture assets is that of a beneficiary of a
constructive trust. (Rosen v. E.C. Losch Company, Inc. (1965) 234 Cal.App.zd 324,335.)
Accordingly, we conclude that, as a joint venturer, Councilmember Kane has a beneficial
economic interest in the other four properties that are assets of the joint venture.
3 See Gross v. Raeburn (1963) 219 Cal.App.2d792,799 (holding that statute of frauds does not apply
when property owners orally agree to combine their properties and form a joint venture.)
Once a public official identifies his or her relevant economic interests, the official must
evaluate whether it is reasonably foreseeable that a decision will have a material financial effect
on one or more of those economic interests. This determination takes three steps which are
discussed in detail below as Steps 4, 5 and 6. First, the official must determine whether the
economic interest will be directly or indirectly involved in the decision. (Regulation
18700(bX4).) Based upon the type of involvement, the official must then find the applicable
materiality standard set forth in Commission regulations. (Regulation 18700(b)(5).) After
finding the applicable materiality standard, the official must then decide whether it is reasonably
foreseeable that the standard will be met. (Regulation 18700(b)(6).)
4. Direct or Indirect Involvement
a. Business Interest
A person, including a business entity, is directly involved in a decision if the person
initiates, is a named party in, or the subject of, the decision. (Regulation 18704.1(a)(2).) A
person is the "subject" of the decision if the decision involves the issuance, renewal, approval,
denial or revocation of any license, permit, or other entitlement to or contract with the person.
Qbid.) If a person is not directly involved in a decision, that person is indirectly involved for
purposes of finding the applicable materiality standard. (Regulation 18704.1(b).)
Neither the joint venture, nor any of its individual members, is a named party in, or the
subjects of, the decision to approve the Hawarden Project. Therefore, the joint venture is
indirectly involved in the decision.
b. Real Property Interest
Real property is directly involved in a decision when the decision involves: (1) the
zoring, sale, lease, or boundary change of the property; (2) a license, permit or other land use
entitlement authorizing a specific use of the property; (3) taxes or fees to be assessed on the
property; or (4) redevelopment, and the properly is located in the redevelopment area.
(Regulation 1870a.2@).) If real property is not directly involved in a decision, it is indirectly
involved for purposes of finding the applicable materiality standard. (Regulation 18704.2(b).)
Here, the councilmember's real property interests are indirectly involved in the decision
to approve a nearby development project.
5. Applicable Materiality Standard
a. Business Interest
Regulation 18705.1(b) sets forth the materiality standards for business entities that are
indirectly involved in a decision. The standards in the regulation vary depending upon the size
of the business entity. The bigger the business entity, the greater the monetary impact must be in
order for the effect to be material.
Presumably, the joint venture is a small business entity as described in Regulation
18705.1(b)(7).4 For such businesses, the effect of a decision is material if it will result in an
increase or decrease in the entity's: (1) gross revenues of $10,000 or more in a fiscal year; (2)
existing expenses of $2,500 or more in a fiscal year; or (3) value of assets or liabilities of
$10,000 or more. (Regulation 18705.1(bX7).)
b. Real Property Interest
Regulation 18705.2 sets forth the materiality standards that apply to economic interests in
real property. The materiality thresholds vary depending upon the distance between the official's
real property and the real property that is the subject of the decision.
(1) Councilmember Kanets Real Property - Councilmember Kane's property is more
than 2,500 feet away from the Hawarden project. When an official's property falls outside a
2,500-foot radius from the boundaries of the proposed project, any financial effect on the
property is not considered material unless BOTH of the following apply: (1) it is reasonably
foreseeable that the project will affect the fair market value of the official's property by $10,000,
or the arurual rental value by $1,000, and (2) the effect will not be substantially the same as the
effect on at least 25 percent of all the properties within 2,500 feet of the official's property; or
there are not at least 10 properties under separate ownership within 2,500 feet of the official's
property. (Regulation 1 8705.2(bX2).)
(2) Joint Venture's Real Property - One of the five properties that is part of the joint
venture falls within 2,500 feet of the Hawarden project. For property interests that are between
300 and 2,500 feet from the boundaries of a proposed project, the effect of the decision will be
material if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will increase or decrease: (1) the fair
market value of the property by $10,000, or (2) the annual rental value of the property by $1,000
(Regulation 1 870s.2(b)( 1 XC).)
a Regulation I 8705. I (bX7) business entities are entities that are not listed on the Pacific Stock Exchange
or the Eligible Securities List maintained by the Departrnent of Corporations, and that do meet the qualifications for
listing on the NASDAQ Stock Exchange. (Regulation 18705.1(b).)
6. Foreseeability
Once the official finds the relevant materiality standard, the official must determine
whether it is reasonably foreseeable that the standard will be met as a result of the decision. A
financial effect is considered reasonably foreseeable at the time a govemmental decision is made
if there is a substantial likelihood that it will occur. @egulation 18706.) A material financial
effect need not be a certainty as a result of the decision, but it must be more than a mere
possibility. (In re Thorner (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. l9S.)
Consequently, the question is whether it is substantially likely that the decision to
approve the EIR for the Hawarden project will have a material financial effect on any of
Councilmember Kane's relevant economic interests.
a. Councilmember Kane's Economic Interest in Joint Venture
As to the joint venture, the specific question is whether it is substantially likely that the
decision will increase or decrease the entity's: (1) gross revenues by $10,000 or more in a fiscal
year; (2) existing expenses by $2,500 or more in a fiscal year; or (3) value of assets or liabilities
by $10,000 or more. (Regulation 18705.1(bX7).)
To answer this question, Councilmember Kane must consider whether the Hawarden
project will materially affect the fair market value of any of the five parcels that are part of the
joint venfure, since these properties are "assets" of the joint venfure. ln addition, she must also
consider whether the project will affect the number of residential units that the joint venture
plans to build, and, if so, whether the resulting effect on the entity's gross income would be
material. (See Willians Advice Letter, No. A-00-036, copy enclosed.)
b. Councilmember Kane's Economic Interest in Her Real Property
Regarding her interest in real property, Councilmember Kane must determine whether
there are special circumstances about the Hawarden project which make it substantially likely
that the decision to approve the project will affect the fair market value of her property by
$10,000 or more, or the rental value of her property by $1,000 or more in a l2-month period.
(Regulation 18705.2(b)(2).) If such circumstances are present, the effect will be material unless:
(1) the effect of the decision will be substantially the same as the effect upon at least 25 percent
of all properties which are within a 2,500-foot radius of the boundaries of her home; or (2) there
are at least 10 properties under separate ownership within a 2,500-foot radius of her home.
You indicate that there are at least ten properties under separate ownership within 2,500
feet from the councilmember's property. Therefore, she does not have a conflict of interest in
decisions relating to the Hawarden project because of her real property interest.
c. Councilmember Kane's Economic Interest in Joint Venture Property
For her beneficial interest in the real property contributed to the joint venture that is
within 2,500 feet of the Hawarden project, Councilmember Kane must determine whether it is
substantially likely that approval of the project will increase or decrease the fair market value of
the real property by $10,000, or the annual rental value of the property by $1,000. (Regulation
18705.2O)(1XC).) If this effect is substantially likely, she will have a conflict of interest in the
project decision.
7. Public Generally Exception
An official who otherwise has a conflict of interest in a decision may still participate in
the decision if the'public generally" exception applies. (Section 87103.) For this exception to
apply, the decision must affect the official's economic interests in substantially the same manner
as it would affect a significant segment of the public. (Regulation 18707.)
Applying the exception is a two-step process. First, the official must determine whether a
"significant segment" of the public will be affected by the decision. Second, he or she must
determine whether this significant segment will be affected in "substantially the same manner" as
the official. When applying the two steps, real property and business interests must be analyzed,
separately. In addition, when an official has multiple economic interests that are the basis for his
or her disqualification in a decision, the official may participate in the decision under the public
generally exception only if the exception applies to each interest.
For an economic interest in a business entity, a significant segment may include 50
percent of all businesses in the official's jurisdiction or district. (Regulation 18707(bX1XB).)
Councilmember Kane is elected at large; therefore, her district is the entire city. You have not
provided any information regarding the number of businesses in the city that will be affected by
the Hawarden project. Therefore, we cannot determine whether the pubtic generally exception
will apply to the councilmember's economic interest in the joint venture.
For an economic interest in real property, a significant segment may include: (1) 10
percent or more of the population of the official's jurisdiction or district; (2) 10 percent or more
of all property owners, homeowners or households in the official's jurisdiction or district; or (3)
5,000 individual residents in the official's jurisdiction or district. (Regulation 18707(bX1XA).)
You have not indicated that ten percent of all the property owners in the city will be affected in
substantially the same manner as Councilmember Kane. As such, the public generally exception
does not apply to her real property interests.
8. Legally Required Participation
The eighth step pertains to the "legally required participation" rule. (See Regulation
18708.) This rule applies only in rare cases where several public officials in the same agency are
simultaneously disqualified. It does not appear that legally required participation rule is relevant
to your request.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: 1. Does Councilmember Kane's involvement in the development of a tract of land, part
of which is owned by her, constitute an interest in a business entity for purposes of the Act?
2. If so, does Councilmember Kane's interest in the business entity create a conflict of
interest that would preclude her from participating in the decision to approve an EIR and a permit
in connection with a residential development project located near the tract?

CONCLUSION: 1. Yes. The joint development of property rises to the level of a joint venture and, as
such, is a business entity as defined in the Act. As a partner in the joint venture, the
councilmember has an economic interest in the business entity for conflict-of-interest purposes
2. The councilmember will have a conflict of interest in a decision relating to the
Hawarden project if the decision will affect the fair market value of the five parcels that are
assets of the joint venture by $10,000 or more, or have any other material financial effect on the
joint venture as described below.
embedding.first_500_words: Farn Polrrrcrl Pnacucps CouvrrssroN P.O. Box tt07 . 42tt J Strec't . Sucramento. CA 9-5till-0U07 (916) 322--t660 . Fax (916) -322-0tt86 July 25,2000 Juanda Lowder Daniel Deputy City Attorney City of Riverside 3900 Main Street Riverside, California 92522 Re: Your Request for Advice Our File No. A-00-134 Dear Ms. Daniel: This letter is in response to your request for advice on behalf of Riverside City Councilmember Maureen Kane about the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").r QUESTIONS 1. Does Councilmember Kane's involvement in the development of a tract of land, part of which is owned by her, constitute an interest in a business entity for purposes of the Act? 2. If so, does Councilmember Kane's interest in the business entity create a conflict of interest that would preclude her from participating in the decision to approve an EIR and a permit in connection with a residential development project located near the tract? CONCLUSIONS 1. Yes. The joint development of property rises to the level of a joint venture and, as such, is a business entity as defined in the Act. As a partner in the joint venture, the councilmember has an economic interest in the business entity for conflict-of-interest purposes I Govemment Code sections 81000 - 91015. Commission regulations appear attitle2, sections 18109 - 18996, of the California Code of Regulations. File No. A-00-134 Page 2 2. The councilmember will have a conflict of interest in a decision relating to the Hawarden project if the decision will affect the fair market value of the five parcels that are assets of the joint venture by $10,000 or more, or have any other material financial effect on the joint venture as described below. FACTS The city council is considering an application for a permit to establish a planned residential development on72 acres of land characterizedby significant rock outcroppings and steep slopes (referred to as the "Hawarden Project"). The project is located in the residential conservation zone. Councilmember Kane owns real property consisting of significant acreage situated approximately 3,200 feet west of the Hawarden Project. There are at least ten parcels with 2,500 feet of the councilmember's real property. In addition, the councilmember and the owners of four large acreage parcels surrounding her property jointly applied to the city for atractmap ("Tract240l6") to subdivide and develop the property. Councilmember Kane has no direct ownership interest in the other four surrounding parcels. However, once the city approves a final subdivision map, all five of the joint venturers will have an economic interest in the combined properties. The easterly most boundary of Tract 24016 is located within 2,500 feet of the Hawarden Project. The Hawarden Project is coming before the city council for discussion concerning the scope of the Environmental lmpact Report ("ER"). Upon completion of the EIR, the Hawarden applicant will request certification of the EIR" and approval of the project. The councilmember's project will not be contingent upon the approval or denial of the Hawarden project. However, due to their
